nativeRange.habitat: Emergent aquatic plant of ponds, lakes and slow-moving streams across the Mississippi River basin, from central Texas and the Florida Panhandle north to southern Illinois.

summary: The Broadleaf Chilensis (Sagittaria platyphylla) is a sturdy, broad-leaved rosette plant native to the Mississippi River basin that spreads readily via runners to form a grassy background stand — a larger, more imposing relative of Sagittaria subulata and a generally forgiving choice for medium to large planted tanks.
careNotes:
The Broadleaf Chilensis is sold in the aquarium trade under several names, including 'Broadleaf/Delta Arrowhead', 'Delta Duck-potato', and 'Giant Sagittaria'. Despite the 'Chilensis' trade name, it is NOT a South American species — it is native to the United States, found growing as an emergent marsh plant across the Mississippi River basin from central Texas and the Florida Panhandle north to southern Illinois.

In aquaria, submerged growth typically reaches 25–50 cm, placing the plant firmly in the background of most layouts. Under very strong light it tends to stay somewhat more compact and can serve as a tall midground accent, but it is considerably larger than its close relative Sagittaria subulata [fw-plant-047] and should be given open space behind other plants to spread out. The species is amphibious — in the wild it produces large, ovate to elliptical arrow-shaped leaves on tall petioles, with the whole plant reaching up to around 150 cm. Open-top tanks and paludariums can allow it to grow emergent and develop this characteristic arrowhead foliage.

The Broadleaf Chilensis is a fairly heavy root feeder. Nutrient-rich planted-tank aquasoil is the preferred substrate, but it also grows well in fine gravel or sand when root tabs are placed nearby and refreshed regularly. Iron supplementation is commonly recommended if the leaves begin to yellow or pale. CO2 injection is generally not required, but in high-energy planted setups it tends to produce denser, more compact leaves and faster runner production. Temperatures between 22 and 28 °C are broadly tolerated.

Propagation in the aquarium happens almost entirely through stolons, or runners — horizontal stems that grow out from the base of the mother plant and produce new rosettes at their tips. A healthy plant will send out runners continuously; the new rosettes can be separated once they have developed their own roots and replanted wherever space allows.

IMPORTANT: this species is a declared noxious weed in Australia and has become invasive in China's Yangtze River basin. Aquarists in those regions should verify local regulations before purchasing and should never release trimmings or plant material into natural waterways.
breedingNotes: The Broadleaf Chilensis propagates very readily by stolons — horizontal runners that emerge from the base of the mother plant, each producing a new rosette at its tip. Once a new rosette has developed its own root system, it can be separated from the runner and replanted in a new location. The wild plant also reproduces by seed, producing small white flowers arranged in a raceme (an elongated cluster on a central stem), and develops underground storage structures called corms, but neither of these methods is a practical propagation route in the aquarium.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Modern Aquarium product page. Lists temperature 72–82 °F (~22–28 °C), moderate–strong light, CO2 not required, easy care level, moderate-to-fast growth, foreground–midground placement, runner propagation, nutrient-rich substrate preferred with root tabs in sand/gravel, iron supplementation if leaves pale. Native range stated as 'North America and Southeast Asia' — partly incorrect; Wikipedia confirms the species is US-native and only invasive in Asia/Australia, so the careNotes record the corrected native range.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Sagittaria platyphylla
additional.notes: Taxonomic and biogeographic cross-check. Confirms family Alismataceae, order Alismatales, native range central Texas–Florida Panhandle north to southern Illinois (Mississippi basin), amphibious growth with emergent ovate-elliptical blades up to 17 cm and overall plant up to 150 cm emergent in the wild, propagation by stolons/seeds/corms, noxious-weed status in Australia and invasive in China's Yangtze basin. Common names: delta arrowhead, broad-leaf arrowhead, delta duck-potato.

additional.name: Splashy Fish — Sagittaria Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked submerged aquarium height (~25–50 cm / 10–20 in), tank size guidance (20+ US gallons → 75 L floor), moderate light recommendation (6,500–7,500 K LED, 8–10 h photoperiod), runner propagation, moderate growth rate, CO2 optional. Resolves the height/placement question — Modern Aquarium's foreground/midground claim disagreed with the user-supplied note that this is a background plant; cross-source consensus on 25–50 cm submerged plus the comparison to S. subulata (10–30 cm) confirms background placement is correct.

propagation: seed (wild only, not practical in aquaria)
